runs.interpretation: The compensated substitution elasticity representing the percentage change in hours worked by primary earners in the second earnings decile for a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage rate within a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation.
citations: Saez, E., 'The Optimal Taxation of Low-Income Families' (2002)
Chetty, R., 'Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions' (2012)
Blundell, R. and MaCurdy, T., 'Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches' (1999)
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical labor supply literature generally finds that primary earners (often heads of households) exhibit very inelastic labor supply behavior. While low-income populations (decile 2) may be more responsive to tax-benefit policy changes due to participation decisions (extensive margin), the intensive margin substitution elasticity remains quite low. Estimates clustered around 0.1 reflect the consensus that compensated elasticities for this demographic are small but positive.
